skill.name: pytdc
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Therapeutics Data Commons workflows for drug discovery datasets, benchmarks, data splits, molecular oracles, and model evaluation.
skill.summary: Use PyTDC datasets, benchmarks, splits, and molecular oracles for therapeutic machine learning workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, C2, weak cryptography, and network indicators, but review found these are mainly markdown inline code, Python examples, biomedical dataset names, SMILES strings, and official PyTDC links. No prompt injection, credential handling, shell execution, or data exfiltration evidence was found in the scanned files. The remaining risk is low because the skill includes runnable example scripts and workflows that may download public datasets through PyTDC.

low_findings.title: Runnable PyTDC Example Scripts
low_findings.description: The skill includes Python examples that load PyTDC datasets, split data, evaluate benchmark predictions, and score molecules with TDC oracles. These scripts do not invoke subprocesses or handle secrets, but they may fetch public datasets through the PyTDC library when users run them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts are present and import PyTDC APIs that normally load datasets or oracles. Review found legitimate educational examples and no shell execution, credential access, or exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: Official Network References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to official TDC documentation, benchmark, and repository resources. This is a legitimate documentation pattern, not a covert network destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are readable official project links and a benchmark submission page. I found no code sending local data, secrets, or credentials to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many backtick-related command execution alerts occur in markdown inline code, fenced Python examples, and dataset catalog entries. They are documentation syntax, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside markdown documentation or Python code fences. There is no Ruby code, shell interpolation, subprocess call, eval call, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Biomedical Keyword Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: C2 and weak-cryptography alerts match biomedical terms, dataset names, molecular strings, oracle names, and model evaluation text. These are domain vocabulary items, not command-and-control behavior or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe HIV datasets, GDSC and MHC datasets, molecular generation oracles, and SMILES examples. No cryptographic API usage, malware infrastructure, or C2 communication pattern was found.

content.value_statement: Therapeutic machine learning requires reliable datasets, splits, metrics, and benchmarks. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use PyTDC workflows for drug discovery research.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to load PyTDC single-instance, multi-instance, and generation datasets.
Guides scaffold, random, cold-drug, cold-target, and custom train validation test splits.
Documents ADME, toxicity, DTI, DDI, molecular generation, and benchmark group workflows.
Shows how to use TDC evaluators for regression, classification, and benchmark reporting.
Provides molecular oracle examples for QED, SA, LogP, GSK3B, DRD2, and related scoring tasks.
Includes reference catalogs for datasets, utilities, and molecular generation oracles.
limitations: It does not install PyTDC or required scientific dependencies by itself.
Example scripts use simple placeholder models and dummy predictions for demonstration.
Dataset availability and API behavior depend on the installed PyTDC version.
Oracle scores are computational predictions and do not replace experimental validation.

use_cases.title: Start an ADME Prediction Baseline
use_cases.description: Load a curated ADME dataset, apply scaffold splits, and prepare train, validation, and test data for a baseline model.

use_cases.title: Benchmark Therapeutic ML Models
use_cases.description: Follow PyTDC benchmark group protocols, evaluate across required seeds, and format reproducible benchmark results.

use_cases.title: Score Generated Molecules
use_cases.description: Use TDC molecular oracles to evaluate generated SMILES strings for drug-likeness, synthesis, and target activity.

prompt_templates.title: Load a Dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pytdc skill to show me how to load an ADME dataset, inspect its columns, and create a scaffold split.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Split Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pytdc skill to compare random, scaffold, cold-drug, and cold-target splits for a drug-target interaction project.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Benchmark Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pytdc skill to design a five-seed benchmark workflow for an ADMET task, including metrics and result reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Objective Oracle Scoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pytdc skill to plan a multi-objective oracle scoring workflow for generated molecules using QED, SA, LogP, and target activity.

output_examples.input: Help me start with Caco2_Wang in PyTDC.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow that loads the Caco2_Wang ADME dataset, applies a scaffold split, identifies label columns, and suggests baseline metrics.

output_examples.input: I need to evaluate a DTI model fairly.
output_examples.output: A split strategy recommendation that explains cold-drug and cold-target evaluation, leakage checks, and suitable affinity metrics.

output_examples.input: Score generated molecules with TDC oracles.
output_examples.output: A molecular scoring plan that combines drug-likeness, synthetic accessibility, lipophilicity, and target-specific oracle scores.

best_practices: Use scaffold or cold splits when molecular or entity generalization matters.
Report benchmark results across the required seeds with mean and standard deviation.
Validate high-scoring generated molecules with domain review and experimental evidence.
anti_patterns: Do not compare models trained on modified benchmark splits against official leaderboard results.
Do not treat oracle scores as confirmed biological activity or clinical evidence.
Do not tune on test data or reuse test labels during feature engineering.

faq.question: What is PyTDC?
faq.answer: PyTDC is the Python interface for Therapeutics Data Commons, a collection of drug discovery datasets, benchmarks, and utilities.

faq.question: Which tasks does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers ADME, toxicity, HTS, DTI, DDI, drug response, molecular generation, retrosynthesis, and related therapeutic ML tasks.

faq.question: Can it train a complete model for me?
faq.answer: It provides workflow guidance and example templates. You still need to choose, implement, and validate your model.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: PyTDC may need network access to download datasets or resolve external resources when examples are run.

faq.question: Are the included scripts safe to run?
faq.answer: The reviewed scripts are educational examples without shell execution or credential handling. Run them in a controlled Python environment.

faq.question: Can oracle scores replace lab testing?
faq.answer: No. Oracle scores are computational estimates and should be validated with expert review and experimental testing.
